mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz CLS320/350 CDI engine (OM642) costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only, varying by generation: C219 £3,000–£5,500, C218 £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. An oil cooler seal replacement costs £800–£1,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: That depends on which generation. The C219 (2004–2010) has well-documented oil cooler seal failures (oil/coolant contamination), swirl flap motor issues, and injector failures. The C218 (2011–2018) benefits from revised injectors, improved electronics, and better swirl flap design. Buy the C218 if you can. The OM642 V6 is a robust engine when properly maintained.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal failure (oil/coolant contamination, £800–£1,500), swirl flap motor failure (loss of low-end torque, £500–£1,200), injector failure (rough idle, misfire, £300–£800 per injector), and DPF/EGR issues (£400–£1,200). The C218 has improved reliability over the C219.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: CLS values range from £3,000–£18,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,000–£5,500 reconditioned engine represents 20–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained C218, replacement is often justified. For an ageing C219 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The CLS320/350 CDI uses the OM642 engine code — Mercedes' 3.0-litre V6 diesel. The C219 (2004–2010) produces 224bhp, while the C218 (2011–2018) produces 265bhp. Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the oil cooler seal issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the oil cooler seals can be replaced (£800–£1,500). This is a well-known OM642 repair. The oil cooler is located in the V of the engine; replacing the seals requires removing the intake manifold. If you see oil in the coolant (mayonnaise in the expansion tank) or coolant in the oil, stop driving immediately and book a specialist inspection. Ignored seal failure can lead to bearing damage and engine failure.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both CLS diesel generations. OM642 used engines are widely available due to the engine's extensive use across the Mercedes range. Reconditioned units for the C218 typically command a premium due to improved specification. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLS320/350 CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The V6 layout is relatively accessible.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — can cause engine failure if ignored
Forced regen or cleaning — worth it

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * C218 (2011–2018) — the more refined and reliable generation
* Documented oil cooler seal history — replacement with updated seals
* Full service history with regular oil changes and Mercedes-approved oil
* C219 with swirl flap motor and injector history
avoidIf: * Any evidence of oil/coolant contamination (mayonnaise in expansion tank) — oil cooler seal failure
* C219 with loss of low-end torque without documented repair — swirl flap motor issue
* Poor service history — the OM642 is unforgiving of neglect
* DPF warning light without documented diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLS320/350 CDI is a stylish, refined four-door coupé with a torquey V6 diesel. The C218 (2011–2018) is a significant improvement over the C219 (2004–2010), with revised injectors, improved electronics, and better overall refinement. The OM642 V6 is a robust engine but has well-documented issues: oil cooler seals, swirl flap motors, injectors, and DPF/EGR systems. Buy the C218 if you can, or a well-maintained C219 with documented work on the key weak points.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz CLS320/350 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz CLS320 / CLS350 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle; the OM642 should start cleanly
* Oil / Coolant Check — Check expansion tank for mayonnaise (oil/coolant contamination — oil cooler seal failure)
* Service History — Check the year to identify which generation — pre-2011 = C219, 2011+ = C218
* Swirl Flap History (C219) — Motor replaced? Flaps checked?
* Injector Performance — Check for smooth idle; injector issues are common on high-mileage cars
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; frequent short trips cause DPF issues
* EGR System — Any coolant loss or white smoke? EGR cooler failure is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
